Long before the great ice sheets melted, the northern skies were an endless, suffocating canvas of black. No moon graced the long winter nights, leaving the creatures of the frost to wander in eternal shadow. Among them was Aethon, a great fox whose fur was the color of the midnight snow. 

Aethon pitied the shivering beasts of the tundra and resolved to steal the lost light of the gods. He journeyed to the highest frozen peak at the edge of the world, where the Sky-Weaver spun the stars. While the Weaver slept, her celestial loom lay abandoned, draped in loose, luminous threads of emerald, violet, and gold. 

Carefully, Aethon caught the glowing strands in his bushy tail and bolted down the mountain. But the Weaver awoke and cast a freezing wind to stop him. As Aethon ran, the mighty gale scattered the starlight from his fur. The threads swept upward, freezing upon the breath of the wind and dancing across the dark heavens. 

Aethon never returned, frozen at the world’s edge, but his daring theft forever banished the absolute dark. To this day, the vibrant ribbons of the Aurora Borealis ripple across the sky, the eternal remnants of the frost fox's flight.

Long ago, before the world had a voice, the high mountains were ruled by a lonely spirit named Oros. Oros was forged of gray stone and eternal frost, massive and unmoving. He watched the rivers flow and the forests grow, but he could never participate in the dance of life because he had no voice. The world around him was entirely silent, save for the rushing wind.

One dawn, a tiny golden bird named Lyra flew to Oros's highest peak. She carried within her the very first song of the sun. As she perched on the giant's shoulder, she opened her beak and poured out a melody so warm and pure that it cracked the ancient ice. 

Oros was so moved by the beautiful sound that he desperately wanted to keep it. He reached out with his deep, cavernous heart to catch the notes. Though his stone form could not create its own music, his hollow caves captured the bird’s melody and bounced it back into the sky. 

Startled, Lyra sang again, and Oros answered. From that day on, Oros was never lonely. Whenever a traveler calls out in the canyons, Oros answers, offering the gift of the Echo.

One evening, a celestial serpent named Kaelen descended from the upper atmosphere. Kaelen was woven of brilliant white fire, darting across the sky in jagged, unpredictable lines. He carried the wild heat of the cosmos, illuminating the plains below.

Now, whenever the iron titan reaches for the sky-serpent, the world witnesses the blinding flash of lightning, followed by the furious roar of thunder.

The stars began to flutter their diamond wings, shedding fine, shimmering dust of starlight. As this cosmic frost drifted downward, it met the biting winds of the upper atmosphere. The stardust ignited into brilliant, dancing ribbons of emerald, violet, and gold. 

These flowing waves of light brushed against Aethon’s crystalline tail, filling the sky with breathtaking color. The world’s first auroras were born—a majestic, glowing bridge between the earthbound frost fox and his celestial companions, proving that true bonds could never be severed by distance.

Myth: The magnificent songbirds brought life and joyous melodies to the great forests, their vibrant feathers flashing like jewels through the lush emerald canopies. For an age, the world was filled with their unbroken chorus. But as the world turned, the first autumn swept across the continents. The warm winds grew biting and cold, and the verdant leaves withered into shades of brown and gold before falling to the earth.

The songbirds, born of fragile ash and delicate petals, began to shiver. Their bright songs faded into sorrowful chirps as the chill threatened to extinguish the lingering embers of Ignis within their tiny hearts. Aero, the wind spirit, saw their plight and wept, his tears falling as freezing sleet. 

Desperate to save his creations, Aero called upon Terra. The earth goddess cracked open the deep southern mountains, revealing hidden valleys warmed by bubbling geothermal springs. Aero then gathered the trembling flocks. With a mighty breath, he created invisible currents in the sky—great, sweeping rivers of wind. 

Guided by Aero’s powerful gales, the songbirds took flight on the world’s first great migration. Today, when autumn chils the air, birds still ride the wind southward to Terra’s warm embrace.

Aethon, the frost fox, watched the isolated campfires dotting the endless white plains like lonely stars. Racing through the frozen valleys, Aethon visited each camp, playfully striking the glowing hearth-stones with his crystalline tail.
